author: @kadmow
text:
the non backdrivability of the hypocycloid drive makes the differential set irreversible to the primary hypocycloid input.

Another use for a differential drive - pure mechanics, is to use (one of many options) an infinitely variable transmission on the low torque input, to self excite the prime mover to generate "infinitely variable" output (reciprocal) torques and speeds - + and -, to Vmax... (industrial torque  "vectoring devices" may use hydraulics traditionally, of course a speed controllable electric motor is also  an infinitely variable input...)

I had a great time making a 2 stage hypocycloidal drive (non balanced) to generate a 2 stage 100:1 ratio in a 1" thick assembly - back in my early 3d printing days.. (using SW, I needed the cartesian form of the hypocycloid wave pattern, that was a bit of a trick to do... - Using a polar system - which I was more used to do (at the time) in Creo, is so much simpler..)

author: @arielmarshall5501
text:
Thank you once againt Robert for sharing this, it is a fantastically useful mechanism. 
I am absolutely devastated atm, I have not got the resources to fully develop my ideas myself yet and in the last week or two, I have seen five or six of my design concepts get "proof of concept" videos uploaded to youtube, including the design I wanted this for, yesterday. I have dated text messages describing the design, from well before the upload, but I doubt I can develop the idea now without getting sued or something. From now on I will not mention my ideas to anyone, as it seems like my phone/internet activities are possibly being monitered and exploited. I would be alright with this if my circumstances did not desperately require me to earn some money due to how horrific they are. Thanks for everything you do anyway.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am sorry to hear that mate - but you could always do computer models and post them maybe?

replies.author: @arielmarshall5501
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Do you mean sharing the design files with the community, for them to print and use in/modify for their designs? I am all for this and everything I design is for the purpose of making this world a safer place for future generations, so I expect to do this a lot more in future, BUT (It's a big but), it's absolutely essential for me to earn some money first. Many of my more beneficial designs will not be cheap to prototype, on top of a number of other reasons. Often one cannot just approach investors, expecting a good deal, without disclosing your idea to them and giving them the opportunity to relay your idea, to an alternative more favorable developer. Also some designs cannot be scaled down in size for testing their functionality in a prototype.
There will be many designs that I will be happy to share with you and your community, but right now my focus is primarily on resourcing all of the tools and the materials that I need, at prices that are within my means.

author: @VeniceInventors
text: Thank you Rob, after watching this video it finally dawned on me that to power my grain mill with an AC motor (which spins way too fast), instead of using a small wheel on the motor shaft connected by a belt to a very large wheel on the mill to get a 60:1 ratio, I can get the same reduction with a much smaller 3D-printed cycloid gearbox.

author: @mrdewilliams
text:
I have been toying around with this differential idea for a couple of years.  I have been looking to design a pedal car that combines the work of the pedalers.  I have been to beaches and other places where they rent pedal cars to groups and families to ride around in.  The trouble with them is that all of the pedals all drive a singular chain.  So a great deal of the muscle work by the strongest pedaler is lost making up for the weakest pedaler.  I am envisioning a pedal car where even the efforts of a small child can add to the efforts of an adult to make the car go.  I see a differential as the best solution for this, although I have yet to determine which design will work best.

I can also see this as a good idea for the little toy pedal cars for children.  You can have a larger child and a smaller child riding and pedaling together, with each feeling like they are contributing to the car but not feeling like they are doing all the work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like your thinking mate and yes a diff would be an answer i think

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: ??- differentials setup  with spragged inputs - to eliminate any reverse rotation (inadvertent back driving) on any unpopulated pedals -  have as many inputs as your system designer can conceive - all summing to feed the one output  (an AWD system  - as many as you want 1,2,3,4,5,6..  etc..  in reverse, spur gear differentials are very suited to constant  duty / operation.)

author: @johnschneider931
text: Just putting ut out into the ether, a good project, a professor in Glasgow creaded a harmonograph .  A dual pendulum platform that can draw a Lissajous curve.  A circle or eclipse can be a special Lissajous curve. The wikipedia details what "angular frequency"  (of the pendulums?) will give you a circle.  So we have a pendulum(s) ,  a pretty graph,  circular or elliptical motion, than we can hook up an input , wind,  and use the motion to turn the stored energy of the pendulum into electricity to power a capacitor and led.  All we need is a really big 3d printer.  Just putting it out there.
